title: Quantum Tunneling and Its Absence in Deep Wells and Strong Magnetic Fields
speaker: Jacob Shapiro
abstract: New results on quantum tunneling between deep potential wells, in the presence of a strong constant magnetic field are presented. This includes a family of double well potentials containing examples for which the low-energy eigenvalue splitting vanishes, and hence quantum tunneling is eliminated. Further, by deforming within this family, the magnetic ground state can be made to transition from symmetric to anti-symmetric. For generic double wells in a certain regime, tunneling is not suppressed, and a lower bound for the eigenvalue splitting is given. Joint with Fefferman and Weinstein.

proof_stages.label: Deep single-well bound states and stable gap
proof_stages.summary: The speaker sets up a one-well Schrödinger Hamiltonian in two dimensions and recalls that, for deep wells, low eigenstates are localized with a non-collapsing spectral gap, as suggested by harmonic-oscillator asymptotics.

proof_stages.label: Nonmagnetic double-well tunneling benchmark
proof_stages.summary: For two distant identical wells, the classically expected double degeneracy splits into symmetric and antisymmetric states, with an exponentially small splitting governed in the nonmagnetic theory by the Euclidean action and proved using WKB or Feynman-Kac large deviations.

proof_stages.label: Strong magnetic double-well formulation
proof_stages.summary: The Hamiltonian is modified by minimal coupling to a constant magnetic field of size proportional to the well-depth parameter, a scaling justified by quantum Hall motivation and by magnetic harmonic-oscillator asymptotics.

proof_stages.label: Magnetic phase obstruction to standard tunneling estimates
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that positivity, magnetic WKB, and real Feynman-Kac large deviations fail, and reformulates the splitting through a magnetic-translation hopping coefficient containing a rapidly oscillatory phase that can cause cancellation.

proof_stages.label: Radial-well positive lower bound
proof_stages.summary: Under radiality of the well and ground state, an explicit radial ODE/Kummer-function representation outside the well allows angular integration to remove the dangerous oscillation and yield a positive lower bound for hopping or splitting.

proof_stages.label: Nonradial zero-tunneling construction
proof_stages.summary: Starting from a radial well with known nonzero hopping, a compactly supported localized perturbation is chosen, via first-order perturbation theory and a winding-number argument, to tune phase and amplitude so that the hopping coefficient or eigenvalue splitting vanishes.

proof_stages.label: Average lower bound via analyticity
proof_stages.summary: Although pointwise lower bounds can fail, analyticity of the hopping/splitting in a complex sector plus a single perturbative-magnetic lower bound gives a quantitative averaged lower bound in the semiclassical parameter, hence failure only on a density-zero set.

examples.name: Harmonic oscillator near a nondegenerate well minimum
examples.note: The exactly solvable oscillator is used to make the deep-well eigenvalue gap and eigenfunction localization concrete, but the later theorems are stated in a more general setting.
examples.engagement_reason: the harmonic oscillator approximation near the well minimum is invoked and its known asymptotic spectrum/eigenfunction behavior is stated, but not actually computed or derived in the talk

examples.name: Left-to-right tunneling of a localized state
examples.note: Starting with a state localized in the left well illustrates that the eigenvalue splitting is also the inverse time scale for transfer to the right well.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker decomposes a left-localized state into symmetric and antisymmetric double-well eigenstates, evolves the phases, and computes the time at which it becomes right-localized

passes.reason: Serves as the usual dynamical interpretation of the double-well splitting, not an adversarial case.

examples.name: Gaussian phase-cancellation toy model
examples.note: Replacing the ground state and wells by Gaussians shows explicitly how the magnetic phase can be tuned to cancel the hopping coefficient, revealing the mechanism behind the zero-tunneling construction.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker sets up a Gaussian ground state and two Gaussian potential terms, plugs them into the hopping integral, and reasons how the displacement-dependent phase can be chosen to cancel the hopping term

passes.reason: A fine-tuned Gaussian phase cancellation makes the hopping/splitting exactly zero, giving a minimal collapsed no-tunneling case against generic tunneling.
The engineered Gaussian phases cancel so the hopping or splitting collapses to the zero boundary case, showing naive positive tunneling can fail.
The engineered Gaussian phase-cancellation setup makes the hopping term or splitting vanish, a collapsed minimal case used to obstruct naive universal tunneling lower bounds.
The tuned Gaussian phase-cancellation model makes the hopping/splitting vanish, an extreme collapsed case used against a universal tunneling lower bound.
The tuned Gaussian phase-cancellation model collapses the hopping/splitting to zero, the minimal boundary case, and is used to show a general nonzero-tunneling expectation can fail.
